metadata.title: Comprehensive Diligence Layout Stress Deck
metadata.author: KPMG LLP
metadata.subject: Quality of Earnings and Diligence Report

slides.title:
Atlas Holdco
Quality of Earnings
Diligence Report
slides.subtitle:
Integrated financial, operational, and working-capital diligence findings
Prepared for investment committee review | March 2026

slides.title: Executive Summary and Investment Context
slides.strapline: Headline findings indicate strong historical growth with moderating margin profile and concentrated customer exposure
body: Atlas Holdco has demonstrated sustained top-line expansion over the last three fiscal years, supported by recurring enterprise contracts and increasing wallet share within core strategic accounts. Revenue quality is directionally strong, yet a growing share of incremental growth has been sourced from a narrower customer base, which elevates concentration risk under downside stress scenarios.

Adjusted EBITDA remains resilient after normalization, although reported margin compression in the most recent period reflects both product-mix changes and cost-to-serve pressure in implementation-heavy engagements. Working-capital efficiency has improved sequentially, but seasonality and billing milestone structures continue to create quarter-end volatility that should be addressed in transaction modeling assumptions.

From a transaction perspective, our current view supports proceeding to confirmatory diligence with a targeted focus on customer durability, contract economics by cohort, and controllability of delivery costs. The valuation range is highly sensitive to margin recovery pace and concentration-adjusted growth, making downside protections and post-close operating plan governance critical to underwriting discipline.
slides.source: Source: Management interviews, trial balance extracts, and synthetic diligence workpapers

slides.title: Reported Performance Trend Summary
slides.strapline: Three-year trajectory shows robust revenue scaling with moderated EBITDA conversion in the current period
body: Revenue increased from $182M to $347M across FY2023 to FY2025, reflecting both volume growth in core accounts and expansion of adjacent service lines. EBITDA growth remained positive over the same horizon, though conversion decelerated in FY2025 as implementation intensity increased and utilization normalization lagged onboarding pace.

While absolute profitability remains strong, recent trend shape suggests the business has moved from pure scale capture into a phase where operational discipline and mix optimization will determine sustainability. This dynamic is typical for platform businesses crossing into broader enterprise deployment, and it should be explicitly reflected in base-case and downside underwriting assumptions.

Confirm customer-level gross margin, isolate one-time delivery burdens, and test whether margin recovery is visible in current run-rate cohorts. Validate that reported growth quality is supported by contract terms and renewal behavior rather than non-repeatable project spikes.

slides.title: KPI Snapshot and Year-on-Year Movement
slides.strapline: Core KPI panel validates current-period scale, margin profile, and conversion efficiency
insights: Scale increased materially across the review period while EBITDA growth remained positive, supporting a resilient headline profile.
Margin shape suggests operational complexity increased in FY2025, so normalization should focus on delivery burden and mix effects.
Retention remains strong overall, but concentration and working-capital cadence should be tested in downside underwriting.
KPI outputs should be tied directly to scenario assumptions used in valuation and debt-capacity analysis.
slides.notes: Note: KPI set is synthetic and designed for comprehensive layout and density validation.

slides.title: Normalization Framework and Scope
slides.strapline: Normalization logic distinguishes recurring commercial economics from transaction-neutralizing adjustments
leftBody: Our approach begins with reported EBITDA and applies a structured classification framework to separate non-recurring items, accounting reclassifications, and owner-specific economics. Adjustments are only retained where support is auditable and recurrence profile is demonstrably inconsistent with forward-state operations.

Each proposed adjustment is evaluated on magnitude, frequency, and causal linkage to core commercial activity. Items that are cyclical yet inherent to the business model are preserved in normalized earnings to avoid optimistic bias in valuation modeling.
All material adjustments require dual support from ledger-level detail and management explanation, with contradictory evidence defaulting to conservative treatment pending confirmatory testing.
rightBody: Class 1 captures demonstrably non-recurring items such as legal settlement recoveries, one-time restructuring severance, and transaction advisory spend. Class 2 covers accounting normalizations including lease presentation alignment and revenue cut-off corrections identified through period-end testing.

Class 3 addresses owner-specific or run-rate distortions including above-market related-party fees, temporary vacancy burden, and launch-phase delivery inefficiencies that are not expected to persist under steady-state operations. Each class is documented with directional impact and confidence rating.
Normalized outputs are fed directly into scenario architecture to ensure bridge transparency from reported results to valuation-ready earnings.

slides.title: Revenue Adjustment Commentary and Rationale
slides.strapline: Narrative details revenue normalization considerations, including cut-off alignment and non-core stream exclusions
body: Deferred revenue release timing in Q4 produced a favorable pull-forward that was normalized to align with underlying service delivery cadence.
One-time implementation accelerators tied to a specific regulatory deadline were removed from run-rate assumptions given low probability of recurrence.
Pilot program receipts from a non-core channel were excluded from normalized base due to uncertain conversion and limited strategic alignment.
Cut-off controls are generally effective but depend on manual review for complex milestone contracts, creating moderate execution risk in peak close cycles.
Post-adjustment growth remains strong, but quality is more concentrated in top-tier clients, requiring durability testing in downside case design.
slides.source: Source: Revenue testing workpapers, contract review, and management walkthroughs

slides.title: Cost and Margin Adjustment Commentary
slides.strapline: Cost normalization isolates non-recurring burdens and clarifies sustainable gross and EBITDA margin trajectory
body: Margin normalization includes removal of clearly non-recurring restructuring costs and transaction-specific diligence spend while preserving recurring implementation burden and expected support ramp for enterprise clients. This treatment results in a conservative yet decision-useful earnings baseline that better reflects forward operating conditions.

Severance and temporary backfill costs linked to a one-time operating model redesign were adjusted out after validating board-approved restructuring plans and completion status. In contrast, elevated subcontractor spend associated with current delivery complexity was retained because it appears structurally linked to the present client mix and therefore relevant to near-term profitability.
Normalized margin lands below headline management targets, indicating valuation upside relies on demonstrable execution against efficiency initiatives rather than multiple expansion alone.
slides.source: Source: Payroll detail, GL extracts, and synthetic margin bridge schedules

slides.title: Adjusted EBITDA Bridge and Scenario Anchors
slides.strapline: Bridge presentation links reported earnings to normalized and scenario-ready EBITDA anchors
slides.heading: Illustrative EBITDA bridge and scenario anchor points
body: Bridge outputs indicate that normalized EBITDA remains materially above reported baseline after neutralizing one-time costs, though upside assumptions should be tempered by retained recurring delivery burden. This supports a base case anchored in gradual margin recovery rather than immediate reversion to historical peak conversion.

Downside and upside cases should flex both revenue quality and execution intensity, as sensitivity is jointly driven by client concentration, pricing durability, and delivery efficiency. The bridge therefore functions as an underwriting control point, ensuring each scenario traces transparently to identifiable operational drivers.
slides.noteSource: Note source: all values are synthetic and included for integrated layout validation

slides.title: Net Working Capital Diagnostic Summary
slides.strapline: Working-capital profile reflects improving collections with persistent quarter-end volatility
leftBody: DSO has trended favorably over the review period as billing controls and enterprise collection cadence improved, yet concentration in milestone-based contracts still creates periodic step-changes in aging. Receivables quality remains acceptable, though exposure to a limited set of large counterparties increases sensitivity to dispute timing.
Aging analysis shows manageable delinquency levels, but long-tail balances are clustered in projects with complex acceptance criteria that can delay formal invoice approval.
Vendor terms have been modestly extended without evidence of distress behavior, supporting stable DPO assumptions in the base case.
rightBody: Inventory intensity is low in absolute terms but has risen as implementation kits and onboarding buffers were pre-positioned for strategic accounts, increasing temporary cash absorption. Deferred revenue remains a positive cash contributor, though recognition mechanics require contract-level scrutiny to confirm sustainability of the float benefit.
Accrual hygiene is generally strong, with only limited true-up volatility observed at quarter close. This supports confidence in cash conversion assumptions once milestone timing is normalized.
NWC target-setting should incorporate explicit seasonality bands and concentration-adjusted stress factors rather than a static historical average.

slides.title: Cash Conversion Trend and Efficiency
slides.strapline: Cash conversion improved overall but remains exposed to contract milestone timing and concentration effects
body: Operating cash flow conversion improved from 74% to 88% over the review period as receivable discipline tightened and non-core spend was rationalized. Improvement is constructive, yet quarter-level dispersion remains notable in periods with heavier implementation launches and delayed acceptance triggers.

Base-case assumptions should recognize continued cash conversion strength while applying moderate volatility buffers to reflect timing risk around key client invoices. Downside case should flex both DSO and milestone slippage to capture plausible stress outcomes.
Strengthen contract administration for acceptance documentation, improve pre-bill readiness protocols, and tighten escalation cadence for high-value disputed balances.

slides.title: Working Capital Driver Table
slides.strapline: Driver-level table supports transaction target-setting and volatility calibration
insights: Receivable and payable movements trend favorably, indicating stronger cash discipline over the diligence horizon.
Deferred revenue growth supports visibility but should be reconciled to contract terms and service-delivery obligations.
Inventory and accrual movements introduce potential timing noise that should be reflected in target-setting guardrails.
Working-capital assumptions should include stress bands for concentration and milestone timing volatility.
slides.notes: Note: Driver profile reflects synthetic period-end balances and ratios for layout stress purposes.

slides.title: Customer Concentration and Churn Signals
slides.strapline: Concentration remains elevated despite strong retention; downside sensitivity centers on top-account durability
body: Top-five customers represent 57% of current revenue, with two anchor accounts alone contributing nearly one-third of total billings. Although these relationships show healthy renewal history, concentration at this level requires explicit downside scenario design and covenant headroom awareness.

Gross and net retention remain favorable, indicating meaningful product stickiness and expansion potential in existing cohorts. However, churn severity is lumpy when losses occur, suggesting account-level transition plans should be part of post-close value-protection strategy.
Underwriting should pair growth assumptions with concentration stress tests and proactive account governance milestones.

slides.title: Pricing and Mix Scenario Sensitivities
slides.strapline: Integrated scenario view highlights valuation sensitivity to price realization and delivery mix
slides.heading: Illustrative scenario matrix and narrative implications
body: The combined view isolates pricing realization and service mix as primary value drivers in the next underwriting horizon. Base case assumes moderate price retention and gradual mix normalization, while downside case incorporates both discount pressure and elevated implementation burden.

A one-point shift in blended gross margin has a disproportionate effect on implied value due to operating leverage and limited fixed-cost flexibility in the near term. Decision quality therefore depends on rigorous validation of price governance and delivery mix controls during confirmatory work.
slides.noteSource: Note source: synthetic sensitivities for diligence-style scenario framing

slides.title: Diligence Workstreams and Ownership Map
slides.strapline: Four-pillar execution plan aligns analytical depth, governance cadence, and decision-readiness deliverables

columns.heading: Financial QoE
body: Finalize normalization bridge, validate revenue quality by contract cohort, and reconcile margin variance drivers to ledger support.
Issue-ready QoE conclusions with base/downside earnings anchors and confidence ratings.

columns.heading: Working Capital
body: Validate seasonal NWC mechanics, stress collection assumptions, and test milestone billing controls for top-value contracts.
Transaction NWC target range with volatility bands and cash-risk mitigations.

columns.heading: Commercial
body: Assess customer durability, churn triggers, pricing governance, and competitive positioning by strategic segment.
Concentration-adjusted growth view with account-level risk/mitigation plan.

columns.heading: Operations
body: Evaluate delivery capacity, utilization recovery initiatives, and controllability of subcontractor and support cost base.
Post-close value creation plan with owners, timing, and measurable milestones.

slides.title: Principal Risks, Mitigants, and Next Steps
slides.strapline: Risk register aligns key underwriting exposures with concrete mitigation actions and governance ownership
body: Customer concentration could amplify downside earnings variance if two largest accounts reduce scope or delay renewals.
Margin recovery may underperform plan if delivery mix remains implementation-heavy beyond modeled transition period.
Working-capital assumptions may be optimistic if milestone acceptance and billing controls do not improve as expected.
Embed account governance cadence with executive sponsor ownership and early-warning churn indicators.
Implement delivery productivity program with weekly utilization and subcontractor cost tracking to protect EBITDA conversion.
Harden billing readiness controls and enforce dispute-resolution SLAs for top-tier contracts to stabilize cash conversion.
Complete confirmatory testing on top ten contracts, finalize downside covenant stress, and align IC memo with scenario-linked valuation ranges.
slides.source: Source: Integrated synthetic diligence findings and workstream planning outputs

slides.disclaimer: This presentation has been prepared solely for illustrative diligence workflow demonstration. The analyses, metrics, and conclusions shown are synthetic examples designed to validate slide architecture, slot coverage, and reporting density in a transaction-style format. No statements herein should be interpreted as factual financial advice, legal advice, or an actual diligence opinion regarding any real entity or transaction.
